era_context: The dawn of the twentieth century was shaped by intensifying imperial competition among European powers, the consolidation of American and Japanese industrial and military strength, and the aftershocks of the Scramble for Africa and the Boxer Uprising in China. The British Empire remained the world's largest but faced challenges from Germany's naval buildup and the ongoing Second Boer War. Across Asia, reformist and nationalist movements were stirring in response to both internal crises and Western encroachment. Technologically, electrification, wireless telegraphy, and the internal combustion engine were transforming industrial societies, while the vast majority of the world's population remained agrarian.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Interior Sub-Saharan Africa beyond colonial administrative records
Central Asian communities under Russian imperial rule
Pacific Islands outside of colonial capitals
Indigenous perspectives in settler-colonial states (Australia, United States, Canada)
Rural Southeast Asia outside colonial administrative centres

events.title: Death of Queen Victoria
events.description: Queen Victoria died on 22 January at Osborne House on the Isle of Wight after a reign of nearly 64 years — the longest in British history to that date. Her death marked the symbolic end of the Victorian era, a period of unprecedented imperial expansion, industrialisation, and social transformation. She was succeeded by her eldest son, who became King Edward VII, inaugurating the Edwardian era. Victoria's death was mourned across the British Empire and much of Europe, where her descendants occupied multiple thrones, earning her the epithet 'Grandmother of Europe.' The transition raised questions about the future of British foreign policy, particularly regarding relations with Germany, whose Kaiser Wilhelm II was Victoria's grandson.
key_figures: Queen Victoria (Queen of the United Kingdom and Empress of India)
Kaiser Wilhelm II (her grandson, German Emperor)

sources.name: Court Circular and official bulletins from Osborne House, January 1901
The Times (London), obituary and reporting, 22–28 January 1901
A.N. Wilson, Victoria: A Life (2014)

events.certainty_note: Extensively documented event with precise date and circumstances.

events.title: Commonwealth of Australia inaugurated
events.description: On 1 January 1901, the six self-governing British colonies of New South Wales, Victoria, Queensland, South Australia, Western Australia, and Tasmania federated to form the Commonwealth of Australia under the Commonwealth of Australia Constitution Act 1900. The inauguration ceremony took place in Centennial Park, Sydney, with Lord Hopetoun sworn in as the first Governor-General and Edmund Barton as the first Prime Minister. Federation created a national parliament while preserving significant state powers. However, the new nation was founded on exclusionary principles: the Immigration Restriction Act 1901 (the 'White Australia Policy') was among the first pieces of legislation passed by the new parliament, and Aboriginal Australians were largely excluded from citizenship rights and the federal franchise.
key_figures: Edmund Barton (first Prime Minister of Australia)
Henry Parkes (deceased by 1901 but architect of federation movement)

sources.name: Commonwealth of Australia Constitution Act 1900 (63 & 64 Vic, c. 12)
Sydney Morning Herald, 1–2 January 1901, reporting on the inauguration ceremony
John Hirst, The Sentimental Nation: The Making of the Australian Commonwealth (2000)

events.certainty_note: Precise date established by legislation and ceremony; extensively documented.

events.title: Australia passes the Immigration Restriction Act (White Australia Policy)
events.description: The Immigration Restriction Act 1901, passed by the new Australian federal parliament in December, established the legislative foundation for what became known as the 'White Australia Policy.' Rather than explicitly naming racial categories (which the British government discouraged), the Act employed a dictation test in any European language, administered at the discretion of immigration officers, to exclude non-white immigrants. This mechanism was borrowed from the colony of Natal's 1897 legislation. The Act reflected widespread racial anxieties among white Australians, particularly directed at Chinese and Pacific Islander labourers, and remained the cornerstone of Australian immigration policy until its progressive dismantlement beginning in the 1960s and formal abolition in 1973.
key_figures: Alfred Deakin (Attorney-General, architect of the Act)

sources.name: Immigration Restriction Act 1901 (Cth), No. 17 of 1901
Commonwealth Parliamentary Debates, 1901
A.T. Yarwood and M.J. Knowling, Race Relations in Australia: A History (1982)

events.certainty_note: Statutory record; exact date of Royal Assent documented.

events.title: Boxer Protocol signed, ending the Boxer crisis in China
events.description: On 7 September 1901, the Qing government signed the Boxer Protocol (辛丑条约, Xīnchǒu Tiáoyuē) with eleven foreign powers, formally ending the Boxer Uprising and the Eight-Nation Alliance's intervention. The protocol imposed crushing terms: an indemnity of 450 million taels of silver (approximately $333 million, more than the Qing government's annual revenue), to be paid over 39 years with interest; destruction of Chinese forts between Beijing and the sea; foreign troops stationed permanently in the capital; and prohibition of arms imports. The indemnity and associated humiliations deepened the Qing dynasty's fiscal and legitimacy crisis, accelerating reform movements and revolutionary agitation that would culminate in the dynasty's fall in 1911–1912.
key_figures: Li Hongzhang (Qing plenipotentiary, who died shortly after signing)
Empress Dowager Cixi (de facto ruler of Qing China)

sources.name: Text of the Boxer Protocol (Peace Agreement between the Great Powers and China), 7 September 1901
Joseph Esherick, The Origins of the Boxer Uprising (1987)
Robert Bickers, The Scramble for China: Foreign Devils in the Qing Empire, 1832–1914 (2011)

events.certainty_note: Treaty text preserved in multiple archives; date certain.

events.title: Theodore Roosevelt becomes President of the United States
events.region: United States (Buffalo, New York / Washington, D.C.)
events.description: On 14 September 1901, Vice President Theodore Roosevelt was sworn in as the 26th President of the United States following the death of President William McKinley, who had been shot by anarchist Leon Czolgosz on 6 September at the Pan-American Exposition in Buffalo, New York. At 42, Roosevelt became the youngest person to assume the presidency. His accession marked a significant shift in American politics: Roosevelt would pursue an aggressive antitrust agenda ('trust-busting'), expand federal conservation efforts, assert American power abroad (the 'Roosevelt Corollary' to the Monroe Doctrine), and champion progressive reforms, fundamentally reshaping the presidency as an activist institution.
key_figures: Mark Hanna (Republican Party boss, wary of Roosevelt)

sources.name: Contemporary press reporting, The New York Times, September 1901
Edmund Morris, Theodore Rex (2001)
Official records of the oath of office, 14 September 1901, Buffalo, New York

events.certainty_note: Precisely documented in official records, press coverage, and abundant contemporary sources.

events.title: Assassination of President William McKinley
events.description: On 6 September 1901, President William McKinley was shot twice by Leon Czolgosz, a Polish-American anarchist, while greeting the public at the Temple of Music during the Pan-American Exposition in Buffalo, New York. McKinley initially appeared to recover but died of gangrene on 14 September. Czolgosz, who claimed to have been inspired by anarchist Emma Goldman, was tried, convicted, and executed by electrocution on 29 October. The assassination — the third of a US president in 36 years — prompted a reassessment of presidential security and contributed to intensified government crackdowns on anarchist organisations, including the passage of the Anarchist Exclusion Act of 1903.
key_figures: Emma Goldman (anarchist leader, arrested but not charged)
Matthew Mann (surgeon who operated on McKinley)

sources.name: Trial transcript, People v. Czolgosz, Erie County Court, September 1901
Eric Rauchway, Murdering McKinley: The Making of Theodore Roosevelt's America (2003)
Contemporary press reporting across major US newspapers, September–October 1901

events.certainty_note: Exhaustively documented event with court records, medical reports, and press coverage.

events.title: First Nobel Prizes awarded
events.description: On 10 December 1901, the fifth anniversary of Alfred Nobel's death, the first Nobel Prizes were awarded in Physics (Wilhelm Conrad Röntgen, for the discovery of X-rays), Chemistry (Jacobus Henricus van 't Hoff, for chemical thermodynamics), Physiology or Medicine (Emil von Behring, for serum therapy against diphtheria), Literature (Sully Prudhomme, French poet), and Peace (shared between Henry Dunant, founder of the Red Cross, and Frédéric Passy, peace activist). The prizes were funded by Nobel's estate as stipulated in his 1895 will. The Nobel Prizes rapidly became the most prestigious international honours in their respective fields, though the selection of Sully Prudhomme over Leo Tolstoy for the first Literature prize was immediately controversial.

sources.name: Nobel Foundation records and ceremony proceedings, 10 December 1901
Alfred Nobel's will, 27 November 1895
Elisabeth Crawford, The Beginnings of the Nobel Institution (1984)

events.certainty_note: Institutional records with precise dates; no ambiguity.

events.title: Guglielmo Marconi achieves first transatlantic wireless transmission
events.description: On 12 December 1901, Guglielmo Marconi claimed to have received the first transatlantic wireless telegraph signal — the Morse code letter 'S' (three dots) — at Signal Hill, St John's, Newfoundland, transmitted from Poldhu, Cornwall, a distance of approximately 3,500 km. The achievement demonstrated that radio waves could follow the curvature of the Earth rather than travelling only in straight lines, a phenomenon later explained by the existence of the ionosphere. The event was a landmark in telecommunications history, though some contemporary scientists and later historians have questioned whether Marconi truly received an identifiable signal given the crude equipment and atmospheric noise, or whether he heard what he expected to hear.
key_figures: John Ambrose Fleming (consultant engineer for the Poldhu transmitter)

sources.name: Marconi's own account published in multiple newspapers, December 1901; later expanded in Proceedings of the Royal Institution, 1902
John S. Belrose, 'Fessenden and Marconi: Their Differing Technologies and Transatlantic Experiments During the First Decade of the 20th Century', International Conference on 100 Years of Radio (1995)

events.certainty_note: Marconi's claim is well-attested in his own reporting and press coverage. However, independent verification was not possible, and some scholars question whether a clear signal was actually received through atmospheric noise with the equipment available.

events.title: Discovery of major oil at Spindletop, Texas
events.description: On 10 January 1901, the Lucas Gusher erupted at Spindletop Hill near Beaumont, Texas, shooting oil over 45 metres into the air and flowing at an estimated 100,000 barrels per day for nine days before it was capped. Spindletop was by far the largest oil strike in history to that date and proved that enormous petroleum reserves existed along the Gulf Coast. The discovery transformed the American oil industry: it broke the near-monopoly of John D. Rockefeller's Standard Oil by creating new competitors (including companies that would become Texaco, Gulf Oil, and Humble Oil, later ExxonMobil), shifted the centre of US oil production from Pennsylvania and Ohio to Texas, and accelerated the transition from coal to petroleum as the dominant energy source for industry and transportation.
key_figures: Anthony F. Lucas (mining engineer who drilled the well)
Pattillo Higgins (prospector who first identified Spindletop's potential)

sources.name: Contemporary press reporting, Beaumont Journal and national newspapers, January 1901
Judith Walker Linsley, Ellen Walker Rienstra, and Jo Ann Stiles, Giant Under the Hill: A History of the Spindletop Oil Discovery at Beaumont, Texas, in 1901 (2002)

events.certainty_note: Precisely documented with geological surveys, drilling records, and extensive press coverage.

events.title: Second Boer War: British concentration camp system and guerrilla warfare
events.region: South Africa (Transvaal and Orange River Colony)
events.description: By 1901, the Second Boer War had shifted from conventional battles (won by Britain in 1900) to a protracted guerrilla phase in which Boer commandos under leaders like Louis Botha, Jan Smuts, and Christiaan de Wet conducted raids against British supply lines. Under Lord Kitchener's command, the British responded with scorched-earth policies — burning Boer farms and crops — and the systematic internment of Boer civilians and Black Africans in concentration camps. Conditions in the camps were catastrophic: overcrowding, inadequate sanitation, and insufficient food led to epidemic disease. Of approximately 115,000 Boer internees, an estimated 28,000 died (most of them children); of the roughly 115,000 Black Africans interned in separate camps, at least 20,000 died, though the true figure is likely higher due to poorer record-keeping.
key_figures: Emily Hobhouse (British humanitarian who exposed camp conditions)

sources.name: Emily Hobhouse, Report of a Visit to the Camps of Women and Children in the Cape and Orange River Colonies (1901)
Fawcett Commission Report (Report on the Concentration Camps in South Africa), 1902
S.B. Spies, Methods of Barbarism? Roberts and Kitchener and Civilians in the Boer Republics, January 1900 – May 1902 (1977)
Elizabeth van Heyningen, The Concentration Camps of the Anglo-Boer War: A Social History (2013)

events.certainty_note: Documented in British military records, parliamentary inquiries, and humanitarian reports. Black African camp deaths less precisely recorded.

events.title: Philippine–American War: ongoing guerrilla conflict and atrocities
events.description: In 1901, the Philippine–American War continued as Filipino forces under Emilio Aguinaldo and regional commanders conducted guerrilla operations against American occupation. On 23 March, Aguinaldo was captured by US forces under General Frederick Funston through a deception operation in Palanan, Isabela. Aguinaldo subsequently issued a proclamation accepting American sovereignty and calling for peace, though resistance continued under other leaders, notably Miguel Malvar in Batangas and Vicente Lukbán in Samar. The massacre of American soldiers at Balangiga, Samar, on 28 September, and the punitive American response under General Jacob H. Smith (who ordered Samar made into a 'howling wilderness'), highlighted the war's brutality on both sides. The conflict caused massive civilian casualties, with estimates of Filipino deaths from warfare and war-related disease ranging from 200,000 to over 1 million.
key_figures: Emilio Aguinaldo (President of the Philippine Republic, captured March 1901)
Jacob H. Smith (US general, Samar campaign)

sources.name: US Senate Document No. 331, 'Affairs in the Philippine Islands' (hearings, 1902)
Brian McAllister Linn, The Philippine War, 1899–1902 (2000)
Stuart Creighton Miller, 'Benevolent Assimilation': The American Conquest of the Philippines, 1899–1903 (1982)

events.certainty_note: Well-documented in US military records and congressional investigations. Filipino civilian casualty estimates vary widely.

events.title: Ashanti Kingdom annexed by the British Empire
events.description: On 1 January 1902 (following military operations throughout 1900–1901), the Ashanti Kingdom was formally annexed as a Crown Colony within the Gold Coast. This followed the War of the Golden Stool (1900), in which the Ashanti resisted British attempts to seize the Golden Stool (Sika Dwa Kofi), the sacred symbol of Ashanti sovereignty. The British had demanded the stool as a symbol of submission; the resulting uprising, led in part by Queen Mother Yaa Asantewaa of Ejisu, was suppressed by British forces. Throughout 1901, British forces consolidated control, and the Ashanti king Prempeh I remained in exile in the Seychelles. The annexation ended centuries of Ashanti political independence and incorporated the kingdom fully into the British colonial system.
key_figures: Yaa Asantewaa (Queen Mother of Ejisu, military leader)
Sir Frederick Hodgson (Governor of the Gold Coast)

sources.name: British Colonial Office records, CO 96 series (Gold Coast correspondence, 1900–1901)
Ivor Wilks, Asante in the Nineteenth Century: The Structure and Evolution of a Political Order (1975)
A. Adu Boahen, Yaa Asantewaa and the Asante-British War of 1900–1 (2003)

events.certainty_note: Documented in British colonial records and Ashanti oral tradition. Formal annexation date is January 1902 but the military and political process unfolded through 1901.

events.title: Cuba becomes a US protectorate under the Platt Amendment
events.description: On 2 March 1901, the US Congress passed the Platt Amendment as a rider to the Army Appropriations Act, stipulating conditions for the withdrawal of US troops from Cuba following the Spanish–American War. The amendment, drafted by Secretary of War Elihu Root, required Cuba to grant the US the right to intervene in Cuban affairs, to lease naval stations (including Guantánamo Bay), to limit Cuban public debt, and to incorporate these provisions into the Cuban constitution. The Cuban constitutional convention reluctantly accepted the amendment on 12 June 1901 under strong US pressure. The Platt Amendment effectively made Cuba a US protectorate and shaped Cuban–American relations — and Cuban nationalist resentment — for decades until its abrogation in 1934.
key_figures: Orville Platt (US Senator, sponsor of the amendment)
Juan Gualberto Gómez (Cuban delegate who opposed the amendment)
Tomás Estrada Palma (future first president of Cuba)

sources.name: Platt Amendment, 31 Stat. 897 (2 March 1901)
Louis A. Pérez Jr., Cuba Between Empires, 1878–1902 (1983)
Records of the Cuban Constitutional Convention, 1901

events.certainty_note: Statutory record with precise date; Cuban constitutional convention proceedings documented.

events.title: Qing dynasty initiates the New Policies reforms
events.description: In 1901, following the catastrophe of the Boxer Uprising and the punitive Boxer Protocol, the Qing court under Empress Dowager Cixi launched the 'New Policies' (新政, Xīnzhèng) reform programme. Edicts issued from the court in exile at Xi'an (before the court returned to Beijing in January 1902) ordered sweeping changes: the modernisation of the military along Western and Japanese lines, reform of the examination system, establishment of modern schools, restructuring of government ministries, and the study of foreign administrative systems. These reforms were more ambitious than the abortive Hundred Days' Reform of 1898 but came too late to save the dynasty; they paradoxically accelerated the dynasty's downfall by creating modernised military units and an educated class whose expectations outpaced the court's willingness to share power.
key_figures: Liu Kunyi (viceroy, co-author of reform proposals)

sources.name: Imperial edicts of 1901 (collected in Da Qing lichao shilu, Veritable Records of the Qing)
Douglas R. Reynolds, China, 1898–1912: The Xinzheng Revolution and Japan (1993)
Mary C. Wright, ed., China in Revolution: The First Phase, 1900–1913 (1968)

events.certainty_note: Documented in imperial edicts and records of the Qing court. Precise dating of individual reforms varies but the programme's initiation in 1901 is well established.

events.title: Oil concession in Persia granted to William Knox D'Arcy
events.description: On 28 May 1901, Mozaffar ad-Din Shah of the Qajar dynasty granted a 60-year oil exploration concession covering approximately 480,000 square miles (three-quarters of Persia) to William Knox D'Arcy, a wealthy British speculator. The concession, negotiated with the assistance of British diplomatic officials, gave D'Arcy exclusive rights to prospect for and exploit petroleum in exchange for £20,000 in cash, £20,000 in shares, and 16% of annual net profits. After years of difficult exploration, the concession led to the discovery of oil at Masjed Soleyman in 1908 and the founding of the Anglo-Persian Oil Company (later BP). The concession became a symbol of foreign economic exploitation in Iran and a central grievance in Iranian nationalism, ultimately contributing to the 1951–53 oil nationalisation crisis.
key_figures: Sir Henry Drummond Wolff (former British minister to Persia, intermediary)
Antoine Kitabgi (Qajar official who helped broker the deal)

sources.name: Text of the D'Arcy Concession, 28 May 1901
R.W. Ferrier, The History of the British Petroleum Company, Vol. 1: The Developing Years, 1901–1932 (1982)
Ervand Abrahamian, Iran Between Two Revolutions (1982)

events.certainty_note: Concession document preserved; date certain.

events.title: Death of Li Hongzhang
events.description: Li Hongzhang, one of the most powerful and consequential officials of late Qing China, died on 7 November 1901 at the age of 78, just weeks after completing negotiations on the Boxer Protocol. Li had been the dominant figure in Qing foreign relations and military modernisation for four decades, serving as Viceroy of Zhili, commander of the Huai Army, founder of China's first modern navy, and chief negotiator of major treaties including the Treaty of Shimonoseki (1895). His death removed one of the few Qing officials with both the diplomatic skill and political authority to manage relations with foreign powers, further weakening the dynasty's ability to navigate the reforms it had just launched.

sources.name: Contemporary press reports and diplomatic correspondence, November 1901
William J. Hail, Tsêng Kuo-fan and the Taiping Rebellion (1927); also Samuel C. Chu and Kwang-Ching Liu, eds., Li Hung-chang and China's Early Modernization (1994)

events.certainty_note: Date and circumstances well documented in Chinese and foreign records.

events.title: J.P. Morgan creates United States Steel Corporation
events.description: On 25 February 1901, J.P. Morgan announced the creation of the United States Steel Corporation through the consolidation of Andrew Carnegie's Carnegie Steel Company with several other major steel firms, including Federal Steel Company and National Tube Company. Capitalised at $1.4 billion (equivalent to roughly $45 billion today and exceeding the entire US federal budget), US Steel became the world's first billion-dollar corporation and the largest industrial enterprise ever created to that point. The deal made Carnegie the world's richest man (he received approximately $225 million for his share) and exemplified the era of corporate consolidation that was concentrating industrial power in the hands of a few financiers, prompting growing public demand for antitrust regulation.
key_figures: Charles M. Schwab (president of Carnegie Steel, later first president of US Steel)

sources.name: New Jersey corporate charter of United States Steel Corporation, 25 February 1901
Ron Chernow, The House of Morgan: An American Banking Dynasty and the Rise of Modern Finance (1990)
David Nasaw, Andrew Carnegie (2006)

events.certainty_note: Corporate and financial records, press coverage, and personal correspondence provide comprehensive documentation.

events.title: Insular Cases: US Supreme Court rules on status of overseas territories
events.description: In a series of decisions in 1901 (Downes v. Bidwell, DeLima v. Bidwell, and others), the US Supreme Court addressed the constitutional status of territories acquired from Spain in 1898 — Puerto Rico, the Philippines, and Guam. The Court established the doctrine that the Constitution did not automatically follow the flag: territories could be governed by Congress without necessarily extending to their inhabitants the full protections of the Bill of Rights. The decisions distinguished between 'incorporated' and 'unincorporated' territories, a framework that effectively created a constitutional basis for American colonial rule. These rulings have had lasting consequences, particularly for Puerto Rico, whose residents remain US citizens without full voting representation in Congress as of the 21st century.
key_figures: Justice Henry Billings Brown (author of majority opinion in Downes v. Bidwell)
Elihu Root (Secretary of War, architect of territorial policy)

sources.name: Downes v. Bidwell, 182 U.S. 244 (1901)
DeLima v. Bidwell, 182 U.S. 1 (1901)
Bartholomew Sparrow, The Insular Cases and the Emergence of American Empire (2006)

events.certainty_note: Supreme Court opinions published in United States Reports; dates certain.

events.title: Karl Landsteiner discovers human blood groups
events.description: In 1901, Austrian physician Karl Landsteiner published his discovery that human blood could be classified into three groups (A, B, and C, the last later redesignated O), based on the presence or absence of specific antigens on red blood cells. A fourth group (AB) was identified by his students the following year. Landsteiner demonstrated that transfusion reactions — often fatal — occurred when incompatible blood types were mixed, explaining why some earlier transfusion attempts had succeeded while others killed the patient. This discovery made safe blood transfusion possible and laid the foundation for modern transfusion medicine, organ transplantation, and forensic serology. Landsteiner received the Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine in 1930 for this work.

sources.name: Karl Landsteiner, 'Über Agglutinationserscheinungen normalen menschlichen Blutes', Wiener Klinische Wochenschrift, vol. 14, pp. 1132–1134 (1901)
Speiser and Smekal, Karl Landsteiner (1975); Nobel Prize biography

events.certainty_note: Published in a medical journal with a precise date. The importance of the discovery was not immediately recognised but the publication record is unambiguous.

events.title: Aguinaldo captured by US forces in the Philippines
events.description: On 23 March 1901, General Frederick Funston led a daring operation to capture Emilio Aguinaldo, president of the First Philippine Republic, at his headquarters in Palanan, Isabela province. Funston's plan relied on Filipino scouts loyal to the US (Macabebe scouts) disguising themselves as insurgent reinforcements. After his capture, Aguinaldo was brought to Manila where, on 19 April, he issued a proclamation acknowledging American sovereignty and urging his followers to lay down their arms. While the capture was a significant blow to the independence movement and was celebrated in the American press as effectively ending the war, armed Filipino resistance continued in multiple provinces for over a year.
key_figures: Emilio Aguinaldo (President of the Philippine Republic)
Lazaro Segovia (Filipino officer involved in the deception)

sources.name: Frederick Funston, Memories of Two Wars (1911)
US War Department records, 1901
Teodoro Agoncillo, Malolos: The Crisis of the Republic (1960)

events.certainty_note: Documented in US military records, press coverage, and Aguinaldo's own later accounts.

events.title: Hay–Pauncefote Treaty permits US-controlled Panama Canal
events.description: On 18 November 1901, the United States and the United Kingdom signed the second Hay–Pauncefote Treaty, superseding the Clayton–Bulwer Treaty of 1850. The new treaty gave the United States the exclusive right to build, control, and fortify an isthmian canal across Central America (eventually built in Panama). Britain, preoccupied with the Boer War and seeking to improve Anglo-American relations, conceded sole American control in exchange for a guarantee that the canal would be open to commercial vessels of all nations. The treaty removed the last major diplomatic obstacle to American construction of the canal and marked a broader British strategic withdrawal from the Western Hemisphere in recognition of growing US power.
key_figures: Lord Pauncefote (British Ambassador to the US)

sources.name: Treaty text, Hay–Pauncefote Treaty, 18 November 1901 (ratified 16 December 1901)
David McCullough, The Path Between the Seas: The Creation of the Panama Canal, 1870–1914 (1977)

events.certainty_note: Treaty text preserved in diplomatic archives; precise dates documented.

events.title: Serious famine in India under British rule
events.description: The Indian Famine of 1899–1900 continued into 1901, affecting an estimated 25 million people across western and central India, particularly in Gujarat, Rajputana, and the Central Provinces. Though mortality figures are uncertain, total excess deaths from the famine cycle of 1899–1902 are estimated at 1–4.5 million. The British response, shaped by Viceroy Lord Curzon's famine policies, was more organised than in the catastrophic famine of 1876–78 but remained constrained by laissez-faire economic ideology, continued grain exports, and punitive work requirements for relief. The famine deepened Indian nationalist criticism of British economic management and became a rallying point for emerging political movements.
key_figures: Romesh Chunder Dutt (Indian economist and critic of British famine policy)

sources.name: Indian Famine Commission Report, 1901
Romesh Chunder Dutt, The Economic History of India Under Early British Rule / In the Victorian Age (1901–1903)
Mike Davis, Late Victorian Holocausts: El Niño Famines and the Making of the Third World (2001)

events.certainty_note: Documented in British colonial records and Indian Famine Commission reports. Mortality estimates vary significantly depending on methodology.

events.title: Japan's Meiji industrialisation: Yawata Steel Works begins production
events.description: On 18 November 1901, the Imperial Japanese Government Steel Works at Yawata (八幡製鉄所) in northern Kyushu began operation, marking a milestone in Japan's industrialisation programme under the Meiji government. Built with German technical assistance and financed partly with reparations from the Sino-Japanese War of 1894–95, Yawata was Japan's first modern integrated steel works and a deliberate assertion of industrial self-sufficiency. Although initial production was plagued by technical problems, the facility became the cornerstone of Japan's heavy industry, producing the steel for naval shipbuilding and railway construction that undergirded Japan's emergence as a military and industrial power.

sources.name: Records of the Imperial Steel Works, Yawata; Japanese government industrial reports, 1901
W. Miles Fletcher, The Japanese Business Community and National Trade Policy, 1920–1942 (1989); see also Kenneth Pyle, The Making of Modern Japan (1996)

events.certainty_note: Government industrial records provide precise commissioning date. Initial technical difficulties are documented in Japanese engineering reports.

events.title: Pan-American Exposition in Buffalo, New York
events.description: The Pan-American Exposition opened on 1 May 1901 in Buffalo, New York, showcasing technological achievements and promoting commercial ties across the Western Hemisphere. The exposition was most notable for its spectacular display of electric lighting — using power transmitted from Niagara Falls — which demonstrated the practical potential of alternating current electricity to millions of visitors. The exposition drew nearly 8 million attendees over its six-month run but is most remembered as the site of President McKinley's assassination on 6 September. The fair also featured ethnological exhibits that displayed indigenous peoples in living dioramas, reflecting the era's prevailing racial hierarchies and imperialist ideologies.
key_figures: William McKinley (attended and was assassinated at the exposition)
Nikola Tesla (whose AC system powered the lighting)

sources.name: Official Catalogue and Guide to the Pan-American Exposition (1901)
Robert W. Rydell, All the World's a Fair: Visions of Empire at American International Expositions, 1876–1916 (1984)

events.certainty_note: Extensively documented in official records, press coverage, and photographs.

disconfirming_evidence: Several commonly held beliefs about 1901 events require qualification. (1) Marconi's transatlantic wireless transmission (1901-008): The claim that Marconi received a clear signal on 12 December 1901 has been questioned by multiple scholars and engineers. John S. Belrose and others have argued that the crude coherer receiver used at Signal Hill could not reliably distinguish the transmitted signal from atmospheric noise at the frequency used. While Marconi unquestionably believed he heard the signal, independent verification was impossible, and the scientific consensus is that the first reliably documented transatlantic transmission occurred in February 1902. (2) Aguinaldo's capture (1901-020): American accounts long presented the capture as effectively ending the Philippine–American War, but modern Filipino and American scholarship (notably by Reynaldo Ileto and Brian Linn) demonstrates that significant armed resistance continued well into 1902 and localised insurgencies persisted until 1913. (3) Spindletop (1901-009): The commonly cited figure of 100,000 barrels per day for the Lucas Gusher may be an overestimate; contemporaneous estimates varied and the figure became inflated in popular retelling, though the strike was unquestionably enormous. (4) The Indian Famine (1901-022): Mike Davis's casualty estimates in 'Late Victorian Holocausts' have been criticised by some historians (notably Tirthankar Roy) as overstated, though the existence and severity of the famine itself is not in dispute. (5) US Steel capitalisation (1901-017): The $1.4 billion figure was the nominal capitalisation and included substantial 'watered stock' — the actual value of physical assets was considerably less, a point emphasised by contemporary critics and later economic historians.
historiographic_note: 1901 is a richly documented year in the modern historical record, with extensive press coverage, government records, diplomatic archives, and personal correspondence available across the industrialised world. However, significant biases shape the surviving record: events are overwhelmingly documented from the perspective of imperial powers (Britain, the United States, European colonial administrations, Japan). African, Southeast Asian, and Pacific Islander perspectives survive primarily through colonial administrative records and later oral history projects. The Philippine–American War, the Boer War concentration camps, and the Indian famine — all events involving massive suffering inflicted or exacerbated by imperial powers — were initially documented primarily through the lens of the coloniser, and revisionist scholarship has worked to recover subaltern perspectives only since the mid-to-late 20th century. Chinese documentation is extensive through Qing court records and regional gazetteers, though the chaos of the post-Boxer period left some gaps.

graph_edges.note: End of Victoria's reign, which began in 1837
The Boxer Protocol was the direct diplomatic resolution of the 1900 Boxer Uprising and Eight-Nation Alliance intervention
The crushing indemnity and humiliation of the Boxer Protocol accelerated the Qing dynasty's collapse
Roosevelt assumed the presidency because of McKinley's assassination
McKinley was assassinated at the Pan-American Exposition
The guerrilla phase and concentration camp system were continuations of the Second Boer War begun in 1899
The military and humanitarian crisis of 1901 shaped the negotiations that ended the war in 1902
Continuation of the Philippine–American War begun in February 1899
The Platt Amendment arose from US occupation of Cuba following the Spanish–American War
The New Policies reform programme was a direct response to the Boxer catastrophe and the Boxer Protocol
The New Policies paradoxically hastened the dynasty's fall by creating modernised elites who demanded more change
The D'Arcy Concession led directly to the 1908 oil discovery and creation of Anglo-Persian Oil Company
US Steel's creation tested the limits of the Sherman Antitrust Act and fuelled the Progressive Era trust-busting movement
The Hay–Pauncefote Treaty removed diplomatic obstacles, enabling the 1903 Panama Canal Treaty
Spindletop accelerated the global transition from coal to petroleum as a primary energy source
Aguinaldo's capture was the most significant single event in the 1901 phase of the Philippine–American War
